      A H IERARCHICAL L ANGUAGE M ODEL D ESIGN F OR
002   I NTERPRETABLE G RAPH R EASONING
003
004
005    Anonymous authors
006    Paper under double-blind review
007
008
009                                                A BSTRACT
010
011            Large language models (LLMs) have seen an increased adoption for tasks with
012            implicit graphical structures, such as planning in robotics, multi-hop question
013            answering, and knowledge probing. However, despite their remarkable success
014            in text-based tasks, LLMs’ capabilities in understanding explicit graph structures
015            remain limited, preventing them from fully replacing Graph Neural Networks
016
               (GNNs) in graph-centric applications. In this work, we introduce a Hierarchi-
               cal Language Model (HLM-G) Design that employs a two-block architecture to
017
               effectively capture local and global graph information, significantly enhancing
018
               graph structure understanding. Our model achieves a new state-of-the-art in graph
019            understanding, outperforming both GNN and LLM baselines. It demonstrates
020            robustness to variations in graph-descriptive prompts, overcoming a key limitation
021            of existing LLMs. Furthermore, we demonstrate the interpretability of our model
022            using intrinsic attention weights and established explainers. Comprehensive evalua-
023            tions across diverse real-world datasets, covering node, link, and graph-level tasks,
024            highlight our model’s superior generalization capabilities, marking a significant
025            advancement in the application of LLMs to graph-centric tasks.
026
027
      1    I NTRODUCTION
028
029
      Large Language Models (LLMs) (Vaswani et al., 2017; Devlin et al., 2018; Achiam et al., 2023;
030
      Chowdhery et al., 2023) have demonstrated impressive generative capabilities, revolutionizing
031   multiple fields, including natural language processing (NLP), computer vision (Wang et al., 2024c;
032   Parashar et al., 2024; Liu et al., 2024b), speech recognition (Fathullah et al., 2024), and cross-modal
033   domains (Wu et al., 2023; Koh et al., 2024). Despite this widespread success, their application to
034   graph tasks remains an emerging area of research (Chen et al., 2024c; Ren et al., 2024; Jin et al., 2023).
035   Unlike linear text data, graph data presents unique challenges due to its non-Euclidean topologies
036   and intricate structures (Jin et al., 2023), making it difficult for LLMs to process these complex
037   relationships effectively. As a result, the adoption of LLMs in graph-centric tasks has been limited,
038   with graph models such as GNNs (Kipf & Welling, 2017; Gilmer et al., 2017) continuing to be the
039
      state-of-the-art in this domain.
040   Applying LLMs to graph tasks presents two key challenges. Firstly, real-world graphs, such as
041   molecules, often consist of complex combinations of features and structures (Qin et al., 2023), such
042   as atoms properties and the bonds between atoms. Although LLMs excel at processing feature-based
043   information due to their strong text comprehension abilities, they often struggle with capturing
044   structural details (Hu et al., 2023). This limitation results in suboptimal performance even on simple
045
      graph tasks, such as identifying shortest paths (Guo et al., 2023; Wang et al., 2024a; Fatemi et al.,
      2023). Consequently, LLMs tend to be effective mainly for node-level tasks, making it challenging to
046
      apply them to more complex link and graph-level tasks where understanding long-range structures
047
      is crucial (Liu et al., 2023; Wu et al., 2021). Secondly, representing graphs using LLMs presents
048   significant scalability challenges (Zhao et al., 2023; Ye et al., 2023b). Describing a graph node with
049   both feature and structural information, as seen in molecular (Dwivedi et al., 2023), citation (Hu
050   et al., 2020b), or knowledge graphs (Dettmers et al., 2018), often results in lengthy prompts, leading
051   to a sharp increase in computational complexity since the attention mechanism in LLMs scales
052   quadratically with input size. This makes the application of LLMs to large graph-based tasks
053   computationally challenging, necessitating specialized designs. On the other hand, a key advantage of
      employing LLMs for graph tasks is their ability to process graphs in a human-comprehensible manner,


                                                          1
      Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2025


054
      allowing input in the form of straightforward text descriptions. Since LLMs use a human-readable
055   vocabulary, they offer a natural advantage in interpretability compared to the opaque embeddings
056   utilized by Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) (Binder et al., 2016; Longo et al., 2024; Achtibat et al.,
057   2024). However, no prior work has focused on providing interpretable results that explain the structure
058   of graphs.
059
      To address these challenges, we introduce Hierarchical Language Model for Graphs (HLM-G), a
060
      novel framework designed to enhance the graph structure comprehension capabilities of LLMs.
061   Unlike conventional LLMs that apply self-attention across all tokens, HLM-G employs a two-block
062   architecture, comprising a local block and a global block, each with specialized attention masking.
063   This hierarchical structure enables the model to initially capture local information in the lower layers,
064   followed by the integration of global-level information in the upper layers. Our approach not only
065   enhances the model’s understanding of graph structures but significantly reduces computational costs,
066   making HLM-G more scalable for large-scale graph tasks. Furthermore, our hierarchical design
067   exhibits increased robustness to variations in graph prompts. We also demonstrate the interpretability
068   of our hierarchical language model with both model intrinsic weights and established explainers.
069
      Finally, we conduct comprehensive experiments across seven real-world datasets, encompassing
      citation networks, knowledge graphs, and molecular graphs. Our results validate HLM-G’s ability to
070
      generalize effectively across node, link, and graph-level tasks, marking a significant advancement in
071
      the application of language models to graph-based tasks.
072
073
074   2    BACKGROUND AND R ELATED W ORK
075
076
      Problem Setup. We denote a graph as G = (A, X, E), where A ∈ Rn×n , X ∈ Rp×n , and
077
      E ∈ Rq×m represent the adjacency, node feature, and edge feature matrices, respectively. Here,
078
      n, m, p, and q denote the numbers of nodes, edges, node features, and edge features, respectively.
079   Building on these, we describe graph tasks in natural language. For each graph Gi , we first construct
080   a sequence Ui that encapsulates the natural language descriptions of Gi covering Ai , Xi , and Ei ,
081   coupled with a query Qi describing the prediction task. Each task is also associated with a true label
082   yi ∈ Y. This leads to a dataset of sequences U = {(U1 , Q1 , y1 ), (U2 , Q2 , y2 ), · · · , (UN , QN , yN )},
083   where each sequence Ui = {u1 , u2 , · · · , uli } and all tokens ui belong to a vocabulary V.
084
      LLM Inference Methods. Prompt engineering has been pivotal in adapting LLMs for a wide range
085   of tasks (Sahoo et al., 2024a; Zhou et al., 2022). Early attempts in prompt engineering for graph
086   tasks involved using structured representations like edge lists and adjacency matrices (Brandes et al.,
087   2013; Zhao et al., 2023), but these struggled with graph structural reasoning tasks (Guo et al., 2023).
088   NLGraph (Wang et al., 2024a) sought to convert graph data into natural language prompts, yet
089   fundamental graph operations remained challenging, even for small graphs. Studies suggest simpler
090   prompts can be more effective, but overall improvements are modest (Zhao et al., 2023; Fatemi et al.,
091   2023; Sahoo et al., 2024b). LLMs continue to underperform compared to specialized graph models,
092   indicating a significant gap (Hu et al., 2023). Beyond prompt engineering other approaches (Yao
093
      et al., 2024; Wang et al., 2022) involves exploring multiple reasoning paths and selecting the most
      confident one, offering marginal gains but at the cost of increased inference time. The limited success
094
      of LLMs on graphs has been partly attributed to their inability to construct coherent world models,
095
      often relying on pattern matching rather than genuine reasoning (Valmeekam et al., 2023; Stechly
096   et al., 2024).
097
098   LLM Fine-Tuning Approaches. Fine-tuning and instruction tuning have been investigated to
099
      address LLMs’ limitations in graph reasoning tasks. Fine-tuning on graph-specific datasets has
      achieved limited success, with models still struggling to capture complex graph structures (Tang et al.,
100
      2023; Vafa et al., 2024). Instruction tuning, which aligns training objectives with graph reasoning
101
      tasks, has shown more promise (Wang et al., 2024b; Luo et al., 2024) by introducing a variety of
102   related tasks during training, enabling the LLM to gain a deeper understanding of the graph domain.
103   However, this approach remains labor-intensive and continues to face challenges with large and dense
104   graphs. Methods such as GraphWiz (Chen et al., 2024a) have further incorporated RL preference
105   alignment (Rafailov et al., 2024), demonstrating some improvements but still struggling on dense
106   graph structures. Furthermore, incorporating real-world graph features, such as node and edge
107   attributes found in citation networks, into LLMs remains an open challenge, indicating that more
      work is needed to fully adapt LLMs for graph tasks.


                                                           2
      Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2025


108
      Hybrid GNN-LLM Approaches. Hybrid models aim to leverage the complementary strengths
109   of LLMs and GNNs by combining the textual understanding capabilities of LLMs with the graph-
110   processing proficiency of GNNs. In this approach, LLMs are often used to enhance graph representa-
111   tions by providing enriched feature descriptions, as demonstrated in models like GIANT (Chien et al.,
112   2021) and LM-GNN (Ioannidis et al., 2022). Alternatively, other methods such as G-Retrieval (He
113   et al., 2024), LLaGA (Chen et al., 2024b), and GraphLLM (Chai et al., 2023) employ LLMs as
114   predictors to improve graph reasoning tasks. Despite their effectiveness, these hybrid models inherit
115   certain limitations associated with GNNs, including the issue of oversmoothing (Rusch et al., 2023)
116   and the need for task-specific architecture designs (You et al., 2020), which require different GNN
117
      structures for node-, link-, and graph-level tasks. Additionally, these approaches face challenges in
      interpretability, as they often rely on opaque embeddings, unlike LLM-only methods that provide
118
      more intuitive, language token-level interpretations. Such token-level interpretability is inherently
119
      more human-understandable and offers clearer insights into the decision-making process
120
121
122   3     H IERARCHICAL L ANGUAGE M ODEL D ESIGN
123
124   In this section, we introduce our Hierarchical Language Model, designed to effectively capture both
125   the structural and feature-based aspects of graphs. We begin by explaining how graph data can
126   be transformed into natural language descriptions (Section 3.1). Following this, we describe the
127
      model’s architecture, which is composed of a local block (Section 3.2) for learning local structural
      information, a pooling layer (Section 3.3) for integrating structural and feature information, and a
128
      global block (Section 3.4) for capturing global information. This hierarchical approach not only
129
      guides our model to better understand graph structures but also results in computational advantages.
130
131
      3.1    NATURAL L ANGUAGE D ESCRIPTIONS OF G RAPHS
132
133   Following prior works (Guo et al., 2023; Fatemi et al., 2023) that demonstrate the effectiveness of
134   using simpler graph inputs for LLMs, we define a graph-to-text representation U to describe any
135   graph task in natural language. For a graph G characterized by its adjacency matrix A, node features
136   X, and edge attributes E, we construct textual representations capturing both node feature and 1-hop
137   structural information for each node vi in G. These representations are divided into two components:
138   the node feature annotation UiX and the node structure annotation UiAE .
139   Node Feature Annotation. Each node can be effectively described in natural language and presented
140   as input to an LLM. The node feature annotation for a node vi , denoted as UiX , is a natural language
141   sequence that describes the attributes Xi of vi over a predefined vocabulary V. The template for UiX
142   is as follows:
143
144       UiX : Node <i> features: <feature 1>: <content 1>; <feature 2>: <content 2>, · · · , <fea-
145       ture p>: <content p>.
146       Example 1 (Citation Network): Node 97 features: Title: A Zero-Knowledge Revocable
147       Credential Verification Protocol Using Attribute-Based Encryption; Abstract: We introduce a
148       zero-knowledge credential verification protocol leveraging Ciphertext Policy Attribute-Based...
149       Example 2 (Molecule): Node 10 features: Atomic Number: 7; Degree: 2; Formal charge: 5;
150
          Number of Hydrogens: 0; Radical electrons: 0; Hybridization: SP2; Aromatic: True; In Ring:
          False.
151
152
      Node Structure Annotation. The node structure annotation UiAE captures the structural connections
153
      of node vi within the graph G, including its connections to other nodes and the corresponding edge
154   features. This serves as a textual representation of A and E. Let ne(i)1 , ne(i)2 , . . . , ne(i)k be the
155   indices of vi ’s 1-hop neighbors in G. The template for UvAE
                                                                i
                                                                   is:
156
157       UvAE  : Node <i> is connected to <ne(i)1 > with <edge feature1 >, <ne(i)2 > with
            i
158       <edge feature2 >, · · · , and <ne(i)k > with <edge featurek >.
159       Example 1 (Citation Network): Node 20 is connected to nodes 10, 14, and 19.
160       Example 2 (Molecule): Node 11 is connected to nodes 10 and 13 by a double bond,. . . and to
161       node 27 by a conjugated double bond.


                                                         3
      Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2025


162
      Task Query. We define a task-specific query Q to represent the prediction task in natural language.
163   This query is tailored for each prediction scenario, as demonstrated below:
164
165     QG : What is the prediction for· · ·
166     Example 1: What is the shortest distance between nodes 0 and 1?
167     Example 2: Does the molecule inhibit HIV virus replication?
168
169   Graph Task Reformulation. Any graph-level task can be reformulated using a concatenation of all
170   nodes’ respective UvXi and UvAE
                                   i
                                      along with the task query Q. Formally, this representation is given
171   by:
172                  f (G) = concat(UG , QG ) = concat(UvAE1
                                                              , UvX1 , . . . , UvAE
                                                                                 n
                                                                                    , UvXn , QG ),
173   where vi ∈ G and concat(·) represents the sequence concatenation operation.
174   While the feature descriptions are generally standardized, there are multiple ways to describe the
175   structural information of a graph. We explore various prompting strategies in Appendix F.2.
176
177   3.2     T HE L OCAL B LOCK
178
179   Since language models cannot inherently understand graphs in their natural structure, we introduce a
180   local-to-global guidance approach, where the model first learns strong local features before capturing
181   information at the global graph level. To implement this, we introduce a local block ML that
182   employs an intra-node attention masking mechanism. This mechanism ensures that, for each node
183
      vi , the combined text sequence (UvAE
                                         i
                                            , UvXi ) is processed independently of other nodes, allowing the
      model to effectively capture node-specific structures and features. Given an input token sequence
184
      H l ∈ Rn×dk at any transformer layer, where n is the total number of tokens across all nodes and dk
185
      is the embedding dimension, we decompose this sequence into segments: H l = {H1 , H2 , . . . , HN },
186   with each segment Hi ∈ Rni ×dk representing the tokens associated with node vi .
187
188   The attention mechanism in the local block is then formulated as:

189      Attention(l) (Q, K, V ) = Diag Attention(l) (Q1 , K1 , V1 ), . . . , Attention(l) (QN , KN , VN ) ,
190
      where
191                                                                                    
                                                         (l)   (l−1)    (l)   (l−1) T
192                                                    (WQ Hi      )(W Hi          )
193
            Attention(l) (Qi , Ki , Vi ) = Softmax               q K                    (W (l) H (l−1)i ).
                                                                                              V
                                                                     (l)
194
                                                                    dk
195
      This block diagonal attention mechanism also provides several computational advantages. Let
196
      nXi and ni
                 AE
                      represent the number of tokens corresponding to the feature annotation UiX and
197                            AE
      structure annotation UiP      for node vi , respectively. The total number of tokens n for the entire
198
      graph is given by n =        ni , where ni = nX       AE
                                                      i + ni . By employing this block diagonal attention
199
      mechanism, we achieve significant computational efficiency compared to traditional  P full    attention
200                                                                                               2
      approaches. In standard attention, the computational complexity is typically O ( ni ) , which
201
202   scales quadratically with the total number of nodes, becoming increasinglyP      expensive   for larger
                                                                                         n2i , resulting in a

203   graphs. In contrast, our block diagonal design reduces the complexity to O
204
      linear scaling relative to the number of nodes. This improvement substantially enhances efficiency,
      especially for larger graph-based tasks, making our approach highly scalable.

      3.3     P OOLING L AYER
207
208   To integrate structural and feature-based information extracted from the graph, we introduce a pooling
209   mechanism. For each node vi , we first derive local embeddings from the hidden states produced by
210
                                                                                                  Pli
      the local block ML . Specifically, the feature-based embedding is obtained as zvXi = n1X j=1       hj ,
211                                                                                                 i

      embeddings through a parameterized pooling operation to produce the final embedding zi for each
214

215

233   Figure 1: Hierarchical Model Design: Local Block employs intra-node attention to learn local node
234   and structural features. Pooling layer combines these features and Global Block utilizes inter-node
235
      attention to capture higher-level interactions, enabling comprehensive graph understanding. The
      Hierarchical model design results in a model which is highly scalable and delivers robust performance
236
      across both structure reasoning tasks and real world graph prediction tasks. The model also supports
237
      dual interpretability: node-level interpretability through the Global Block and fine-grained token-level
238   interpretability via the Local Block, making it not only powerful but also transparent in its predictions.
239

241   where α ∈ (0, 1) is a trainable parameter that balances the contribution of structural (zvAEi
                                                                                                    ) and
                X
242   feature (zvi ) information. A larger α emphasizes the structural properties in the final prediction,
243   while a smaller α gives more weight to feature-based characteristics.
244   Our adaptive pooling mechanism allows our model to work for tasks requiring varying levels of
245   structural and feature importance, such as link and graph-level tasks that demand greater structural
246   emphasis and node-level tasks that rely more heavily on feature-based information. We ablate
247   alternative pooling strategies and configurations, which are detailed in Appendix F.

      level interactions between nodes and enriching the representation with more nuanced graph-level

      4       E XPERIMENTS
289
290
      In this section, we conduct experiments to investigate four specific research questions (RQs) to
291
      assess the effectiveness of our model on graph tasks: RQ1: Can our model accurately understand the
292   underlying structures and maintain robust performance across different graph reasoning datasets?
293   RQ2: Does our approach enhance interpretability performance and produce intrinsic interpretable
294   results? RQ3: Can the proposed method handle complex real-world datasets with diverse node or
295   edge features? RQ4: Does the proposed method work well across all node, link and graph level
296   tasks?
297
298   4.1     S TRUCTURE U NDERSTANDING C APABILITIES OVER G RAPH R EASONING DATASETS
299
300   To answer RQ1, we aim to validate whether our model can process graph structure information by
301   conducting the following experiments on graph reasoning datasets.
302   Datasets. First, following Wang et al. (2024a), we create a synthetic dataset consisting of seven
303   graph reasoning tasks to assess the structural reasoning capabilities of our model. These datasets
304   were constructed by a Random Graph Generator capable of generating graphs with up to 40 nodes
305   and 700+ edges. Further information on these datasets is provided in Appendix C.1.1.
306
      Baselines. We compare our method against both GNN-based and LLM-based approaches. On the
307   GNN side, our comparisons include models such as GCN (Kipf & Welling, 2017), GAT (Veličković
308   et al., 2017), and the more expressive GIN (Xu et al., 2018), as well as the graph transformer model,
309   GTN (Yun et al., 2019). For LLMs, our inference-only methods include Zero-Shot (Huang et al.,
310   2023), Chain of Thought (CoT) (Wei et al., 2023), CoT Self Consistency (CoT-SC) (Wang et al.,
311   2022), and Natural Language Graph (NLGraph) (Wang et al., 2024a) prompting. Additionally,
312   fine-tuning baselines such as BERT (Devlin et al., 2019) and Lora-Trained (Hu et al., 2021) Llama 3
313   are used for direct comparisons. We include GraphWiz (Chen et al., 2024a) as a representative of
314   instruction tuning. The GraphToken (Perozzi et al., 2024) method, which utilizes a GNN encoder to
315
      fine-tune a frozen LLM, is also compared. Detailed information on our experimental configurations
      and hyperparameters can be found in Appendix C.2. For LLMs, we use Llama-3 8B (Dubey et al.,
316
      2024) as the primary backbone.
317
318
      4.1.1     Q UANTITATIVE C OMPARISONS .
319
320   Our method demonstrates state-of-the-art performance across all graph reasoning datasets, signif-
321   icantly outperforming all baselines, both GNN and LLM-based models. Notably, GNNs such as
322   GIN, despite their theoretically strong expressiveness as validated by the WL-1 test (Huang & Villar,
323   2021), struggle with graph-level tasks, failing to match the comprehensive understanding offered
      by our model. Prompt engineering approaches, like CoT and NLGraph and exploration based ap-


                                                                      6
      Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2025


324   Table 2: Structural Robustness Assessment. This table displays the accuracy drop observed over 10
325   permutations for each task. Lower performance drop (↓) indicates less sensitivity to node description
326   positions, highlighting the model’s ability to learn graph structure effectively.
327
       Method               Node Degree(↓)   Edge Existence(↓)   Shortest Distance(↓)   Reachable(↓)   Cycle(↓)   Edge Count(↓)   Components(↓)
328
       NLGraph                   46.1              38.1                 56.6                44.1         49.4         71.6            71.3
329    BERT                      71.4              11.0                 46.2                14.3          9.4          7.8            62.8
       LLaMA 3                   21.5              11.9                 28.9                 8.6         15.9         44.8            62.2

332
333
334   proaches like CoT-SC do not yield substantial improvements in performance, particularly on tasks
335
      like shortest distance that demand a deeper comprehension of the graph structure. GraphToken, a
      hybrid GNN-LLM approach shows limited gains, indicating that using GNN-augmented LLMs is
336
      insufficient for achieving top performance in graph tasks.
337
338   While instruction-tuned models like GraphWiz exhibit better results on smaller graphs, they face
339   significant challenges with larger and denser graphs. Notably, their performance is strong on graphs
340   with up to 100 edges, reaching accuracies of 93% and 84% for the reachable and edge count tasks,
341
      respectively. However, this accuracy drops sharply to 76% and 38% when the graphs become denser,
      with up to 700+ edges, as shown in Table 1. Our model remains highly effective in these dense
342
      scenarios, maintaining near-perfect accuracies across all tasks, demonstrating its robustness against
343
      graph complexity and density. Fine tuning a similar sized BERT and even 80X larger models like
344   Llama-3 is unable to outperform our architecture, underscoring the fact that our design is better suited
345   for graph based tasks than the traditional design.
346
347   4.1.2     E VALUATION OF M ODEL ROBUSTNESS .
348
349   A critical question emerges from the quantitative comparisons: Do language models truly understand
350   graph structures, or do they rely on pattern-matching? To investigate this, we conducted a robustness
351   evaluation by systematically shuffling the node indices of each graph using a permutation matrix P .
352   Unlike GNNs, which are inherently invariant to changes in node indexing due to their symmetrical
353
      message-passing framework, LLMs may exhibit sensitivity to even slight alterations in node token
      representations, potentially leading to inconsistent predictions for the same graph described differently.
354
      This issue highlights a significant shortcoming of LLMs in graph-based tasks.
355
356   In this experiment, we applied the permutation matrix P 10 times to each graph, generating modified
357   adjacency matrices At = P At−1 P T at each iteration t. This process preserves the overall graph
358   structure while changing the node indices, allowing us to evaluate whether the model’s predictions
359
      remain consistent under different representations.
360   The results, presented in Table 2, highlight a stark difference between traditional LLM-based models
361   and our proposed HLM-G model. NLGraph’s performance dropped significantly, indicating that
362   prompt engineering is not robust. Similarly, we observed that fine-tuned LLMs, such as Llama 3 and
363   BERT, exhibited performance drops of up to 21% and 71%, respectively, on the Node Degree task.
364   This highlights their high sensitivity to changes in node tokens and suggests a reliance on pattern
365
      recognition rather than a true comprehension of the underlying graph structure. Instruction tuning
      does not seem to provide robustness as Graphwiz also shows similar sensitivity as finetuned Llama 3.
366
      In contrast, our HLM-G model displays exceptional robustness, with minimal performance drops
367
      (e.g., a mere 6.1% drop on the Shortest Distance task and 0.0% on the Node Degree task). These
368   findings underscore a crucial advantage of our HLM-G, while conventional LLMs struggle with
369   variations in graph representation, our model remains robust, reinforcing its suitability for real-world
370   graph tasks where representations might vary but the underlying structure remains unchanged.
371
372   4.2     I NTERPRETABILITY C OMPARISONS
373
374   Having established the performance and robustness of our model, we now delve into analyzing its
375   interpretability—specifically, its ability to accurately identify and prioritize the most critical structural
376   elements within graph reasoning tasks, thus addressing RQ2. Interpretability serves as a vital criterion
377   in evaluating whether a model is capable of comprehending graph structures rather than just fitting
      patterns. For this purpose, we utilize four graph reasoning datasets that offer explicit ground truths

                                 k                                            k                                             k                                             k
386
387   Figure 2: Explainer Based Interpretation Comparisons. This figure illustrates the interpretability
388   performance of BERT, GIN, and our method on 4 graph reasoning datasets with reasoning ground
389   truths. k indicates the k most important nodes that interpreted by the model are selected.
390
391
392   regarding which nodes are genuinely important for a given graph task. For example, in the shortest
393   distance task, the ground truth consists of nodes that lie along the shortest path between two specified
394   nodes. More details about these ground truths are provided in Appendix E.1. We compare the true
395   structure understanding capabilities of four finetuned models from Table 1: Llama-3, BERT, GIN and
396   HLM-G.
397   To measure interpretability, each model is expected to generate an ordered set r = {r1 , . . . , rn }
398   for a graph with n nodes, ranking them from the most to the least significant, based on the model’s
399   internal focus and reasoning. The quality of a model’s interpretation is then evaluated by how
400   effectively it identifies the nodes that align with the ground truths. Ideally, a model with true structural
401   comprehension should consistently rank ground truth nodes higher, indicating that it genuinely
402   understands the critical elements of the graph structure. Using established explainers, we first reveal
403
      the extent to which our approach successfully captures and prioritizes the essential graph components.
      We then introduce the intrinsic interpretability mechanism built into our model, demonstrating its
404
      ability to provide ready made interpretations.
405
406
407
      4.2.1              E XPLAINER - BASED I NTERPRETATION
408   To objectively compare the interpretability performance across different models, we leverage estab-
409   lished explainability techniques such as Saliency (Simonyan et al., 2013), Input x Gradient (Shrikumar
410   et al., 2016), DeepLIFT (Shrikumar et al., 2017), and GNNExplainer (Ying et al., 2019). This ap-
411   proach allows us to assess how well each model can identify and rank important graph elements,
412   providing insight into the structural modeling capabilities of these models. More details on this
413   strategy, referred to as “explanations as interpretations”, are outlined in Appendix E.2.
414   Setup. To quantify interpretability, we use a Recall@k metric, which measures how effectively a
415   model identifies the nodes that correspond to ground truths. Given a set of ground truth nodes r gt
416   and the set of top-k nodes identified by the model r k = {r1 , . . . , rk }, we calculate Recall@k as
                         k    gt
417
      Recall(k) = |r |r∩r  gt |
                                 |
                                   , where | · | represents the cardinality of the set and ∩ denotes the intersection.
418
      As shown in Figure 2, we evaluate each model’s performance by plotting the recall curve for
419
      k ∈ {1, 2, . . . , n}, where n represents the total number of nodes in the graph. Ideally, a model with
420   a strong understanding of graph structure will have high recall values across different values of k,
421   indicating that it consistently identifies the most important nodes.
422
423
      Results and Analysis. Figure 2 presents the interpretability results for the four models across the four
      graph reasoning datasets. Our HLM-G model demonstrates superior interpretability, particularly as k
424
      increases, indicating a higher proficiency in pinpointing the most relevant nodes for each task. While
425
      GIN performs adequately on tasks requiring simpler one-hop reasoning, such as Edge Existence and
426   Node Degree, it struggles with more complex, multi-hop reasoning tasks like Shortest Distance and
427   Reachability. In contrast, BERT and LLaMA consistently fail to identify relevant structural features,
428   reflecting their limited capability to capture intricate graph patterns. Directly fine-tuning LLMs has
429   not led to significant improvements in these cases. Although Llama 3 outperforms BERT on three out
430   of four tasks, it still does not reach the performance level of GIN or our model. Our model, in fact,
431   excels across all tasks, even those involving multi-hop reasoning, which further confirms its strong
      understanding of graph structures beyond simple pattern matching.

      Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2025

440   Figure 3: Layer-by-Layer Attention Interpretation. This figure compares the mean attention scores
441   for relevant nodes with irrelevant nodes across each layer of the model in 4 graph reasoning tasks.
442   The increased scores in higher layers emphasizes the model’s capability to learn larger scale structure
443   information and identify relevant graph nodes effectively.
444
445   4.2.2    I NTRINSIC ATTENTION I NTERPRETATION
446
447   A key strength of our model design is its inherent interpretability, distinguishing it from existing
448   methods. The local embedding matrix Z (0) in the local block captures 1-hop subgraph information,
449   where each zvi ∈ Z (0) represents the 1-hop ego-graph centered around node vi . As the transformer
450   layers progress in the global block, they progressively integrate this localized information to capture
451   broader global structures within the graph. This means that embeddings in the higher layers reflect
452
      increasingly comprehensive structural details. The attention weights associated with the task query
      node in the global block provide a direct interpretation of the contribution of each node’s structural
453
      information to the final prediction, effectively acting as importance scores for each node. This allows
454
      for a direct, interpretable insight into how the model makes its decisions.
455
456   To illustrate this, we analyze the mean attention scores across all layers, as shown in Figure 3.
457   As we move to higher layers, the attention scores for ground truth nodes increase, while scores
458
      for other nodes decrease. This pattern directly confirms that our model effectively focuses on the
      most important nodes, demonstrating its ability to capture larger-scale structural information. These
459
      attention-based interpretations offer clear insights into the model’s decision-making process without
460
      requiring additional explanation techniques.
461
462
463   4.3     G RAPH L EARNING A BILITY ON R EAL -W ORLD DATASETS .
464
465
      Datasets. To answer the RQ3 and RQ4, we curated seven graph datasets widely recognized in the
      graph learning community, varying in scale, domains, and task types. We adopt Arxiv (Hu et al.,
466
      2020b), Cora (Bojchevski & Günnemann, 2018), and Pubmed (Sen et al., 2008) for node-level tasks;
467
      Pubmed, WN18RR (Bordes et al., 2013), and FB15k-237 (Bordes et al., 2013) for link-level tasks;
468   and molhiv (Hu et al., 2020a) for graph-level tasks. More dataset details are discussed in Table 7.
469
470   Baselines. We compare with traditional GNNs including GCN (Kipf & Welling, 2017),
471
      GAT (Veličković et al., 2017), GIN (Xu et al., 2018) and GraphSage (Hamilton et al., 2017). For
      graph transformer-based baseline we include GTN (Yun et al., 2019) and Graphormer (Ying et al.,
472
      2021). For LLMs, we compare Zero-shot and Few-shot performance using GPT 3.5 (Ye et al., 2023a)
473
      for node-level tasks, and Llama-2-7B finetuned InstructGLM (Ye et al., 2023b) for both node and
474   link-level tasks. For the graph-level task, we compare with a GNN-LLM hybrid model Momu (Su
475   et al., 2022) for molecular graphs. Note that we use Mamba (Gu & Dao, 2023) as a baseline for
476   graph-level task as no Transformer-based LLM is computationally feasible for training on real-world
477   graph-level tasks. OFA (Liu et al., 2024a), a hybrid GNN-LLM model, is also selected as a baseline
478   due to its strong performance on link-level tasks.
479
      Quantitative Results. As demonstrated in Tables 3, 4, and 5, our method consistently delivers
480   competitive performance across node, link, and graph-level tasks. Compared to traditional GNNs,
481   our model surpasses their performance for both node and link-level tasks with large margins. In
482   comparison to hybrid GNN-LLM methods, our model notably outperforms the recently developed
483   LLM-equipped OFA, on link-level tasks where OFA is considered especially strong. Furthermore, our
484   model consistently perform favorably against LLM instruction tuning approach - InstructGLM across
485   link level tasks. Although graph transformers perform slightly better in the graph-level task because
      of their specialized encodings for graph-level tasks, our model produces much higher performance

514   than them in node-level tasks. It is noteworthy that while LLM-only models excel in node-level tasks,
515   they experience a marked decline in performance on link-level tasks, validated their limitations in
516   processing structural information mentioned in Section 2. A crucial factor in our model’s adaptability
517   across all task levels is our pooling parameter α discussed in detail in Appendix F.1. This enables
518   our model to adjust its reliance on structural or feature-based information, thereby allowing it to
519   generalize well across all levels. Our model’s ability to dynamically adjust α provides a significant
520   advantage, making it more versatile and capable of handling a wide range of graph-centric tasks.
521   Overall, our experiments highlight our model’s computational efficiency (Appendix D.3), ability to
522   process structural information, interpretability, and effectiveness across diverse tasks. For additional
523   experiments and ablation studies, please refer to Appendix D and Appendix F respectively.
524
525
      5   C ONCLUSIONS AND D ISCUSSIONS
526
527   In this paper, we introduce a novel Language Model Design to tackle the complexities of non-
528   Euclidean structures commonly found in graphs. While language models excel in text-centric
529   applications, they often struggle with the intricate structures of graph data, leading to significant
530   information loss and computational challenges. Additionally, the context length, which involves the
531   natural language description of a graph, can become enormous for real-world datasets, rendering
532   them ineffective for graphs. Our method sets itself apart by designing a hierarchical architecture
533   to process the graph structure and enhance computational efficiency and interpretability. We show
534   that our model yields promising results in graph reasoning tasks as well as robust and consistent
535
      performance on real-world datasets, outperforming most models designed for similar purposes.
536   This work paves the way for future research in language models for graph learning, establishing a
537   solid foundation for innovation and providing valuable insights into this emerging field. Our findings
538   significantly narrow the gap between conventional language models and graph data, expanding the
539   potential applications and improving the effectiveness of language models in handling structured data.
      We hope this work can shed light on the future direction of LLM-based graph learning.

      Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2025

918
      A    B ROADER I MPACTS
919
920
      Our research aims to enhance the understanding of graph structures through language models (LMs),
921
      marking a modest but significant step toward improved graph reasoning capabilities. This foundational
922   effort seeks to refine how LMs interpret complex graph data, aspiring to inspire further research
923   in this domain. Given the exploratory nature of our work, we have not identified specific negative
924   societal impacts or potential for malicious use directly attributable to our research. Nevertheless, we
925   recognize that all technological advancements carry inherent risks.
926
      In alignment with responsible research practices, we suggest continuous monitoring of developments
927
      in the application of LMs to graph data analysis. As these models evolve to handle more complex
928   tasks, maintaining vigilance becomes crucial to preemptively address any emerging risks before they
929   manifest. Our commitment to ethical conduct underpins our research methodology, which is designed
930   to avoid harm and does not involve human subjects, thus mitigating potential ethical concerns related
931   to privacy and fairness. By promoting ongoing assessment and adopting a proactive approach to
932   research governance, we aim to ensure that our contributions positively impact the field and adhere to
933   the highest standards of ethical research.
934
935
      B    R ELATED W ORKS
936
937
      Graph Neural Networks (GNNs). Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) have emerged as a powerful
938
      framework for learning over graph-structured data (Kipf & Welling, 2017; Gilmer et al., 2017;
939
      Veličković et al., 2018; Wu et al., 2020; Liu et al., 2020). GNNs operate by iteratively aggregating
940   information from a node’s neighbors, thereby learning node representations that capture the local
941   structure and features of the graph. This message-passing mechanism enables GNNs to be highly
942   effective in tasks such as node classification, link prediction, and graph classification. However,
943   despite their success, GNNs are often challenged by issues such as over-smoothing in deeper
944   networks (Rusch et al., 2023) and difficulties in handling long-range dependencies (Sanford et al.,
945   2024), which can limit their effectiveness on larger and more complex graphs.
946   Graph Transformer (GT). Graph Transformers (GTs) (Yun et al., 2019; Rampášek et al., 2022)
947   represent a more recent approach that aims to capture global dependencies within graph data using
948   self-attention mechanisms. Inspired by the success of transformers in NLP tasks, GTs adapt the
949   self-attention mechanism to graph-structured data, allowing them to capture both local and global
950   interactions simultaneously. This approach helps address some of the limitations of GNNs in learning
951   long-range dependencies. However, Graph Transformers often require additional architectural
952   complexities (Black et al., 2024), such as centrality encoding, edge features, and spatial encodings, to
953   effectively represent graph structures. These added complexities can lead to increased computational
954
      demands and make them less interpretable compared to conventional GNNs.
955   Transformer Block in Language Models. In a transformer model, each block processes an input
956   sequence Hi = {h1 , h2 , . . . , hni } to output an updated sequence Hi+1 . A transformer block is
957   structured around an attention mechanism and a feedforward network, both supplemented by residual
958   connections and layer normalization. The multi-head
                                                        T    attention mechanism processes the sequence Hi ,
                                                        QK
959   formulated as Attention(Q, K, V ) = softmax √d           V where Q, K, V are queries, keys, and values
                                                           k
960   derived from Hi , and dk is the dimension of keys. This output is then combined with the original
961   input Hi and normalized: Outputattention = LayerNorm(Hi + Attention(Hi )). Following this, a
962   position-wise feedforward network processes each position in Outputattention , described by FFN(x) =
963   max(0, xW1 + b1 )W2 + b2 with W1 , W2 , b1 , b2 as the network parameters. The final output Hi+1 for
964   the block is computed by applying another layer normalization on the summation of the feedforward
965   network output and the attention output: Hi+1 = LayerNorm(Outputattention + FFN(Outputattention )).
966   This architecture allows the transformer to capture and process dependencies across the input
967   sequence, enabling deep contextual understanding that propagates through successive layers of the
968
      model.
969   Comparisons to Prior Work. LLM-only methods commonly fail to effectively learn from graph
970   data due to computational feasibility and the loss of graph structural information. In contrast, our
971   model addresses these challenges with a local-to-global hierarchical design that efficiently leverages
      graph structure. Hybrid GNN-LM approaches typically encounter problems with task-specific designs


                                                        18
       Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2025


972
       and limited interpretability. In comparison, our method is inherently task-agnostic and demonstrates
973    high interpretability. When compared to closely related conventional Graph Transformers, which
974    necessitate complex designs for centrality, edge, and spatial encoding, our method streamlines
975    the process by exclusively using natural language input, eliminating the need for these elaborate
976    encodings.
977

979
       C       E XPERIMENT D ETAILS
980
       C.1      D ETAILS ABOUT THE DATASETS
981
982    In this section, we describe in detail the datasets used for our experiments. We first describe the
983    Graph Reasoning dataset followed by the real world datasets.
984
985    C.1.1      G RAPH R EASONING
986
987    Several works have proposed benchmarks for graph reasoning, such as the NLGraph (Wang et al.,
988    2024a) and GraphQA (Fatemi et al., 2023). However, upon closer examination, we observed that
989    these benchmarks suffer from significant class imbalance, with some classes having far more data
990
       points than others. For example, in the cycle dataset of GraphQA, 82% of the data samples contain at
       least one cycle. Some works like Graphtoken (Perozzi et al., 2024) have leveraged this dataset, with
991
       their proposed architecture achieving 83% accuracy on the cycle dataset. This raises concerns about
992
       whether the models are truly reasoning on the datasets or simply making majority label predictions.
993    Additionally, the majority of graphs in these benchmarks have a small number of nodes, typically
994    ranging from 5 to 20. In reality, we expect real-world graph datasets to be much larger than this.
995
996
       To address these issues, we propose a new benchmark constructed using a random graph generator.
       Importantly, all datasets in our benchmark are balanced, enabling us to evaluate the true graph
997
       reasoning ability of language models accurately. Training and validation graphs contain up to 40
998
       nodes with test set containing exactly 40 nodes. .
999
1000   In this section we describe our random graph generator used for creating graph reasoning datasets.
1001
1002   Pre-defined graphs To ensure that our generator is well covered, we first include common graphs
1003   including Cyclic graphs, Star graphs, Complete graphs, Path graphs, Tree graphs, Wheel graphs and
1004   Barbell graphs. All of these graphs can be created using NetworkX documentation1 .
1005
1006   Random graphs A graphon is a function W : [0, 1]2 → [0, 1] that takes 2 values v1 ,
1007   v2 ∈ [0, 1] for each pair of nodes and returns the probability p ∈ [0, 1] for an edge between these 2
1008
       nodes. The function W can be any function that takes 2 values v1 , v2 ∈ [0, 1] and returns p ∈ [0, 1] .
       Given two values v1 and v2 , we implement following functions:
1009
1010            1. Constant graphon: Returns a random number p ∈ [0.3, 0.7]
1011
1012
                2. Sparse graphon: Returns a small random number p ∈ [0.05, 0.15]
1013            3. Dense graphon: Returns a big random number p ∈ [0.8, 1.0]
1014            4. Linear graphon: Returns p = v1 ∗ v2
1015
                5. Quadratic graphon: Returns p = v12 ∗ v22
1016
                                                         1
1017            6. Sigmoidal graphon: Returns p = 1+exp(−10(u−v))
1018
                7. Step graphon: Returns p = 1 if v1 ≥ t and v2 ≥ t for some random threshold t ∈ [0, 1]
1019
1020            8. Sin graphon: Returns p = sin(πv1 ) · sin(πv2 )
1021            9. Avg graphon: Returns p = (v1 + v2 )/2
1022           10. Exp. decay graphon: Returns p = exp(−(v12 + v22 ))
1023
1024           11. Softmax graphon: Returns p = exp(vexp(v 1)
                                                      1 )+exp(v2 )
1025
           1
               https://networkx.org/documentation/stable/index.html


                                                          19
       Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2025


1026
       The value vi for the ith node is randomly initialized for each node. Using these formulations, we
1027   prepare our benchmark for structural reasoning tasks. For every task, we extract a graph from our
1028   Random Graph Generator and assign it a label depending on the task. We collect equal number of
1029   graphs for every label to prevent bias towards majority class.
1030
1031                  Table 6: Summary of Graph Analysis Tasks and Their Dataset Specifications
1032
                            Distance   Cycle Detection   Edge Count   Reachability   Edge Existence   Connected Components   Node Degree
1033
        #Classes               6             2              70            2               2                   38                 39
1034    Dataset Size Used    20000          4000           14000         4000            4000                19000              8000
1035
1036   Armed with the general-purpose graph dataset generator, we adapt synthetically generated graphs
1037   to various graph reasoning tasks with varying complexities and describe the problem setups in a
1038   structured manner. Specifically, we first generate subsets of base graphs for each task by controlling
1039   node quantity and graph density. We then tailor these base graphs for specific tasks and design queries
1040   to assess the models’ capabilities accordingly. These 7 datasets summarized in Table 6 are detailed
1041   below. A random split of 80/10/10 is used for training , validation and test sets.
1042
                • Task 1: Shortest Distance
1043
                  Given a graph G = {V, E}, predict the shortest distance between two nodes vi and vj ,
1044
                  categorized into six classes from 0 to 5. Class 0 indicates no path exists, while classes 1 to 5
1045              represent distances from 1 to 5 edges. The query posed is: “What is the shortest distance
1046              between nodes vi and vj ?”
1047
                • Task 2: Cycle Detection
1048
                  In a graph G = {V, E}, determine if a cycle exists. The task classifies graphs into two
1049              categories: presence or absence of a cycle. The question asked is: “Is the graph cyclic?”
1050
                • Task 3: Edge Count
1051
                  Our random graph generator can produce graphs with over 700 edges. To minimize the
1052
                  required training size, we categorize sets of 10 edges into a single class. Specifically, graphs
1053              with 1 to 10 edges are classified as class 0, 11 to 20 as class 1, continuing in this manner up
1054              to 691 to 700, which are classified as class 80. In Section 6, we explore a similar generation
1055              task that features 700 distinct classes (with no grouping of graphs), and it demonstrates
1056              comparable performance.
1057            • Task 4: Reachable
1058              In a graph G = {V, E}, predict whether there is a reachable path between two nodes vi and
1059              vj . The query for this task is: “Are nodes vi and vj reachable from each other?”
1060
                • Task 5: Edge Existence
1061              Determine if an edge exists between two nodes in a graph, represented as G = {V, E}. The
1062              posed query is: “Does an edge exist between nodes vi and vj ?”
1063
                • Task 6: Connected Components
1064
                  Predict the number of connected components in a graph G = {V, E}. A component is a set
1065              of nodes that are reachable from one another. Specifically if vi ∈ C1 and vj ∈ C2 where
1066              C1 and C2 are different components then there exists no path from v1 to v2 . The query is:
1067              “How many connected components does the graph have?”
1068
                • Task 7: Node Degree
1069              Estimate the degree of a node in the graph, representing the number of direct connections or
1070              neighbors the node has. The question is: “What is the degree of node vi ?”
1071
1072   C.1.2      R EAL WORLD DATASETS
1073
1074   We conduct experiments on 7 different Text Attributed Graph (TAG) datasets. All of these graphs
1075   have node features available in natural language. The datasets are concisely summarized in the table
1076
       below, followed by detailed descriptions of each dataset.
1077   Cora dataset, sourced from the GitHub repository as described in Chen et al. (2024c) , is a citation
1078   network in the computer science domain. Each node in this dataset represents a research paper,
1079   with raw text features consisting of the paper’s title and abstract. The edges between nodes indicate
       citation relationships. Nodes are labeled according to the category of the paper, encompassing

       seven possible classes. For our study, we focus on node-level prediction, specifically predicting the

1097   PubMed dataset comprises 19,717 scientific publications from the PubMed database, specifically
1098   related to diabetes, categorized into one of three classes. The citation network includes 44,338 links.
1099
       Each node represents a research paper, with raw text features including the paper’s title and abstract.
       Our study involves both node classification and link classification tasks on the PubMed dataset.
1100
       The raw text data of PubMed dataset was collected from GitHub repository provided in Chen et al.
1101
       (2024c).
1102
1103   For node classification, we use a 60-20-20 random split for training, validation, and testing. For
1104   link classification, the goal is to predict whether two nodes are directly connected. Following the
1105
       methodology of OFA (Liu et al., 2024a), we use an 85-5-10 random split. In the link classification
       task, The training, validation and testing set is created using existence edges as positive samples and
1106
       an equal number of negative samples by checking for the absence of an edge between nodes. The
1107
       evaluation metric for the link-level task is the AUC.
1108
1109
1110   ogbn-arXiv2 is a directed graph representing the citation network among Computer Science (CS)
1111
       arXiv papers. The task involves predicting the 40 subject areas of these papers, such as cs.AI, cs.LG,
       and cs.OS, which are manually labeled by the authors and arXiv moderators. We follow the standard
1112
       split for this dataset: training on papers published until 2017, validating on those from 2018, and
1113
       testing on papers published since 2019. The raw text data of the ogbn-arxiv was collected using the
1114   same protocol as the GitHub repository provided in Prodigy (Huang et al., 2024).
1115
1116   Molhiv3 dataset is a molecular property prediction dataset adopted from the MoleculeNet (Wu et al.,
1117
       2018). The dataset contains 41127 molecules each represented as a graph with atom as nodes and
       bonds as edges. Each atom has 9 discrete features. 1 of the features (Chirality) is common for all
1118
       atoms and is therefore not considered. The rest of the features are: Atomic Number, Degree of atom,
1119
       Formal charge, Number of connected Hydrogen, Radical electrons, Hybridization, Aromaticity and
1120   Ring. These features can be converted to natural language using only a few lines of code. Similarly
1121   the bonds between any 2 atoms can be of 4 types: single, double, triple or aromatic. Each of these
1122   bonds also has a boolean property: conjugated. Therefore any edge can be represented using the
1123   bond type and whether or not it is conjugated.
1124
       Here we perform graph level classification where objective is to classify a molecule as HIV inhibitor
1125
       or not. The metric used here is AUC.
1126
1127
1128   WN18RR is a link prediction dataset created from WN18, which is a subset of WordNet. WN18RR
1129
       dataset contains 93,003 triples with 40,943 entities and 11 relation types. Here we perform link
       classification where we classify any edge in 11 possible edge types. This dataset is extracted from
1130
1131

          3
              ogbg-molhiv is released under license MIT.

       Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2025


1134
       GitHub repository4 .
1135

1137   FB15k-237 is a knowledge graph that contains knowledge base relation triples and textual mentions
1138
       of Freebase entity pairs. It contains 310,116 triples with 14,541 entities and 237 relation types. Here
       we perform link classification where we classify any edge in 237 possible edge types. The raw text
1139
       data of nodes in FB15K237 was collected from the same Github repository as WN18RR.

1142
       C.2     T RAINING AND O PTIMIZATION S ETTINGS
1143
1144
       For the graph reasoning datasets, we train our model from scratch, with the input being the natural
1145
       language description of the graph structure. In the local block, we employ a BERT-like architecture
1146
       utilizing a special intra-node masking scheme that masks out language tokens belonging to different
1147   nodes. Across all reasoning datasets, we use 4 local block layers. For the global block, we utilize 2
1148   layers for most datasets, except for the Shortest Distance, Edge Count and Number of Connected
1149   Components datasets, where 3 global block layers are used. Our observations indicate that more
1150   complex tasks benefit from an increased number of global block layers, which enhances overall
1151   performance.
1152
       We adopt the Adam optimizer (Kingma & Ba, 2014) throughout the training phase, with a learning
1153   rate of 5e−6 , weight decay of 0.1, β1 = 0.9, and β2 = 0.95. Across all datasets, the training consists
1154   of 5 epochs, with a batch size of 16 for graph reasoning datasets and 8 for real-world datasets. The
1155   shared parameters for all tasks and datasets used in our language model ML (G) are summarized in
1156   Table 8.
1157
1158
                                Table 8: Parameters used for the language model.
1159
1160
                                       Parameter                        Value
1161
1162                                   Activation                       gelu
                                       Attention Dropout                 0.1
1163
                                       Dimension                         768
1164                                   Dropout                           0.1
1165                                   Hidden Dimension                 3072
1166                                   Max Position Embeddings          4096
1167                                   Number of Heads                    12
1168                                   Number of Local Block Layers       6
1169
1170
1171   We attempted to leverage pretrained models such as BERT (Devlin et al., 2018), SBERT (Reimers
1172   & Gurevych, 2019), DistilBERT (Sanh, 2019), and Llama 2 7B (Touvron et al., 2023) as the lower
1173   block, but found no performance gains on graph reasoning tasks; in fact, performance declined when
1174
       these models were not fine-tuned. This suggests that these pretrained models do not acquire graph
       structure-related information during pretraining. Our experiments indicate that fine-tuning just 4
1175
       layers of the lower block is sufficient to achieve state-of-the-art performance on graph reasoning
1176
       tasks.
1177
1178   For real-world datasets (Tables 3, 4, and 5), we employ DistilBERT in the local block and fine-tune
1179   it. Given that these datasets contain textual node and edge features, pretrained models are better
1180
       equipped to understand these features. The number of higher block layers for each dataset is set as
       follows: 4 for Cora, Pubmed, WN18RR, and FB15k-237, 2 for molhiv, and 6 for Arxiv.
1181
1182   We observed that using larger models yields improved performance on node-level tasks, as depicted in
1183   Table 9. This is expected since node features play a more critical role in making accurate node-level
1184   predictions within real-world datasets and these larger models are better equiped to understand these
1185   text based features.

1196   C.3   S OFTWARE AND H ARDWARE
1197
1198   Our implementation is under the architecture of PyTorch (Paszke et al., 2019) and PyG (Fey &
1199   Lenssen, 2019). The deployment environments are Ubuntu 18.04 with 48 Intel(R) Xeon(R) Silver
1200
       4214R CPU @ 2.40GHz, 755GB Memory, and graphics cards NVIDIA RTX A6000.
1201
1202   D     A DDITIONAL E XPERIMENT R ESULTS
1203
1204   D.1    D OWNSTREAM TASK P ERFORMANCE
1205
1206   To assess the adaptability and transferability of our proposed model across different graph domains
1207   and task levels, we evaluated its performance on downstream tasks. Specifically, we examined how
1208
       well the model, when trained on one task level (e.g., node, link, or graph), could adapt to perform
       effectively on another.
1209
1210   Experimental Setup: We pretrained our model on three distinct datasets representing different task
1211   levels: Arxiv (Node-level), Molhiv (Graph-level), and Pubmed (Link-level). Each pretrained model
1212   was then fine-tuned on a variety of downstream tasks by updating only the final classification layer
1213   for 5 epochs with a learning rate of 4e−5 . This setup allowed us to evaluate the model’s ability to
1214
       leverage learned knowledge and adapt to completely different downstream tasks.
1215   Results and Analysis: The results in Table 10 demonstrate the impressive transferability of our
1216   model. For example, the model pretrained on the Arxiv (Node-level) dataset achieved an 87.8%
1217   accuracy on the PubMed Link task, outperforming the performance of fully trained GIN despite being
1218   trained exclusively on node-level information initially. Similarly, the model pretrained on the Molhiv
1219   (Graph-level) dataset delivered competitive results on both node-level (Cora) and link-level (PubMed)
1220
       tasks, showcasing its ability to adapt to diverse task requirements.
1221   These insights highlight the versatility of our approach, indicating that our model can effectively
1222   generalize knowledge from one graph domain to another. Our language model design not only
1223   captures graph structures efficiently but can also be fine-tuned for a wide range of downstream
1224   applications with limited training, making it a valuable asset for practical real-world applications.
1225
       Table 10: Downstream task performance with different pretraining datasets. The model’s performance
1226
       was evaluated after fine-tuning only the classification layer for 5 epochs.
1227
1228
              Pretrained \Downstream     Cora (Node)   Pubmed (Node)     Pubmed (Link)    Molhiv (Graph)
1229
              Arxiv (Node)                  80.6            83.8              87.8             72.2
1230          molhiv (Graph)                73.9            75.4              86.6              -
1231          Pubmed (Link)                 71.6            77.5               -               72.5

1233
1234   D.2    G ENERATION TASKS
1235
       The current architecture employs local and global transformer blocks and a classification layer for
1236
       final prediction. For generation on graphs, we need a Decoder model that can generate the output.
1237
       For this, we take inspiration from GraphLLM (Chai et al., 2023) and leverage Prefix-Tuning (Li &
1238   Liang, 2021) for fine-tuning a Frozen Decoder LLM with HLM-G encoder.
1239
1240   Prefix Tuning Given a pre-trained LLM with an L-layer transformer, prefix tuning prepends K
1241
       trainable continuous tokens (prefixes) to the keys and values of the attention at every transformer layer.
                                                                                              M
       Taking the l-th attention layer as an example (l < L), prefix vectors Pl ∈ RK×d is concatenated


                                                          23
       Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2025


1242                                       M                          M
       with the original keys Kl ∈ R∗×d and values Vl ∈ R∗×d , where dM is the dimension of LLM,
1243   formulated as:
1244
1245                                                                               M

1246
                                   Kl′ = [Pl ; Kl ] ; Vl′ = [Pl ; Vl ] ∈ R(K+∗)×d
1247
       The new prefixed keys Kl′ and values Vl′ are then subjected to the l-th attention layer of LLM. For
1248   simplicity, we denote the vanilla attention computation as Ol = Attn(Ql , Kl , Vl ). The computation
1249   of attention becomes:
1250
1251
                                        Ol = Attn(Ql , [Pl ; Kl ] , [Pl ; Vl ])
1252
1253   We introduce three distinct datasets tailored for graph generation tasks, each with unique complexities
1254   and requirements. These tasks are designed to evaluate the model’s ability to generate graph structures
1255   and properties accurately.
1256
1257   • Task 1: Shortest Path
1258     The objective of this task is to generate the shortest path between two specified nodes in a graph.
1259     Given a graph G, the query QG is formulated as: ”What is the shortest path from node i to j?”,
1260
         where i and j are valid nodes within G. The output is considered correct only if the path generated
         is both valid and the shortest possible.
1261
1262   • Task 2: Bipartite Detection
1263     This task aims to determine whether a given graph is bipartite. A graph is defined as bipartite if it
1264
         contains no odd cycles. The challenge for the model is to predict if the graph is bipartite or, if not,
         to generate an odd cycle. The query QG is: ”Is the graph bipartite?”. An output is deemed correct
1265
         if it accurately predicts whether the graph is bipartite or identifies an odd cycle when the graph is
1266
         not bipartite.
1267
1268
       • Task 3: Edge Count
         This dataset involves predicting the exact number of edges in a graph, enhancing the edge count
1269
         task detailed in Section 4. Unlike the previous version, this task does not classify edges into pooled
1270
         groups but requires an exact count. Additionally, the training set does not include all edge counts
1271     present in the test set, introducing unseen scenarios. The query QG is: ”What are the number of
1272     edges in the graph?”. Correctness is strictly judged on the model’s ability to match the exact number
1273     of edges in G.
1274
1275   Table 11: Performance comparison for zero shot and HLM-G encoder on different generation tasks.
1276   Llama-2 7B is used as a decoder in both settings. (across 1 random run).
1277
1278                                        Shortest Path    Bipartite Detection    Edge Count
1279
                        Zero shot                5.2%              11.7%                2.1%
1280                    HLM-G encoder           93.4%              95.1%               92.5%
1281
1282   This data indicates that HLM-G has substantial potential as a powerful graph encoder. The high
1283   accuracy across different tasks in our tests demonstrates its effectiveness. Further experiments are
1284   necessary to fully explore the zero-shot and few-shot capabilities of HLM-G. These future studies will
1285   help validate the model’s performance across a broader range of graph-based applications, potentially
1286   establishing HLM-G as a useful tool in for leveraging LLMs on graphs.
1287
1288   D.3    C OMPUTATIONAL E FFICIENCY
1289
1290   We systematically compare the training efficiency across various LLM-based methods on graph
1291   reasoning datasets and real world dataset.
1292   Graph Reasoning Datasets. Our study evaluates multiple fine-tuning approaches, which we catego-
1293   rize into two primary groups: Hybrid GNN-LLM fine-tuning and LLM-only fine-tuning. We present
1294   training times for GraphToken (a hybrid method), BERT, Llama 3 (LLM-only), and our proposed
1295   HLM-G model (LLM-only fine-tuning). GraphToken utilizes a 4-layer GCN as its GNN encoder
       with approximately 5.2 million training parameters, resulting in a total parameter count of around 8


                                                            24
       Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2025


1296
       billion, comparable to the Lora-trained Llama 3. For BERT, we adopt a 4-layer architecture with four
1297   attention heads, yielding 56 million parameters. The parameter count for our HLM-G model varies
1298   depending on the dataset, comprising 82 million parameters for tasks such as distance, edge count,
1299   and the number of components, and 77 million for reachability, cycle, and edge existence datasets.

1313   dual-block architecture significantly reduces both the training time per epoch and the total time to
1314   convergence. In contrast, GraphToken, while achieving shorter training times per epoch, requires
1315   a substantially higher number of epochs to reach convergence due to its use of a GCN encoder.
1316   Additionally, the training times for Llama 3 are notably high, as expected, due to the model’s
1317   extensive number of parameters and the maximum input prompt length of 4096, which necessitates
1318   longer training durations. In comparison, our HLM-G model exhibits considerable improvements
1319
       in training efficiency, highlighting the computational advantages of our approach, especially in
       managing large-scale graph reasoning tasks.

1324   incorporates Lora with a rank of 16, resulting in 8.2 million trainable parameters. The trainable

       parameter count for Mamba is approximately 91.8 million. For HLM-G, the number of trainable

1326

       process such data more effectively. This design enables HLM-G to handle the intricate details and

       E     I NTERPRETATION
1356
1357
       E.1    I NTERPRETATION G ROUND T RUTH
1358   In the context of graph reasoning datasets, any graph can be partitioned into two distinct sets of nodes:
1359   citical ground truth nodes, which are directly responsible for the final prediction, and non-critical
1360   nodes, which do not influence the prediction either directly or indirectly. Due to the importance of
1361   focusing on structurally relevant nodes, we exclude datasets such as components and edge count where
1362   each node is integral to the final prediction. This exclusion is crucial as it allows us to experimentally
1363   investigate our model’s attention mechanisms towards nodes that are truly significant in the reasoning
1364   process. Detailed ground truth sets for 3 link-level and 1 node-level task are described below.
1365   Edge Existence In the edge existence task between two nodes u and v, the nodes u and v themselves
1366   are sufficient for determining the presence of an edge, thus forming the ground truth:
1367
1368
                                                       GT = {u, v}
1369
       Shortest Distance For the shortest distance between nodes u and v, ground truth nodes include all
1370
       nodes lying on any shortest path. Let l be the shortest path length, then ground truth is simply union
1371
       over all these nodes:
1372                                            ml
                                                [
1373                                    GT =       {u, ai1 , ai2 , . . . , ail−1 , v}
1374                                               i=1
1375   where ml is the number of shortest paths.
1376   Reachability Dataset Unlike the shortest path dataset, reachability requires consideration of all
1377   nodes in all possible paths from u to v, including those beyond the shortest path. If n is the total
1378   number of nodes in the graph, the ground truth set includes:
1379                                                mj
                                                n−1
1380                                            [ [
                                        GT =              {u, ai1 , ai2 , . . . , aij−1 , v}
1381
                                                 j=l i=1
1382
1383   where ml is the number of paths of length j, j ∈ {l, l + 1...n − 1}. This represents a more holistic
1384   understanding of the graph’s connectivity by including paths of length l through n − 1.
1385   Node Degree For node degree tasks focused on a single node u, the determination of degree relies
1386   solely on its direct connections to other nodes in the graph.. The ground truth is straightforward in
1387   this case:
1388                                                GT = {u}
1389
1390   Together, these definitions facilitate a comprehensive evaluation of our model’s capability to handle
1391
       various structural reasoning tasks, each necessitating a specific set of nodes as ground truth based on
       task requirements.

       E.2    E XPLANATION AS I NTERPRETATION
1395
       It is challenging to compare interpretability performance with methods that are not interpretable or
1396   have different interpretation formats. To achieve such comparisons, we propose to use explanations
1397   of models as interpretations. 5 However, explanations provided by explainers face their possible
1398   performance issue that the produced explanations might not be faithful to the deep model behaviors.
1399
       This faithfulness issue requires us to first discover the most faithful explanations for the models
1400
       before using them as model interpretations. Therefore, instead of using one explainer, we adopt four
1401
       explainers to select the best explanation for each model on each dataset including Saliency (Simonyan
1402
          5
1403        Note that explainers provide post explanations that can be applied to any models, while interpretations are
       generally produced by the model’s specific design, a.k.a., self-interpretable model.

1511   choose the right explainer for each model on each dataset. With the best explanation, we use it as the
       interpretation of the model to conduct interpretability comparisons mentioned in Section 4.2.2.
