       E.3   I NTERPRETATION V ISUALIZATION
1567
1568   We present the interpretation results in Figures 5 and 6. As depicted, important tokens are highlighted
1569   with a green background. The methods under comparison are required to count the nodes connected
1570   to node 0 for making predictions. While our method accurately processes this task, BERT fails to
1571   correctly identify the relevant node for degree counting. This discrepancy arises because node 0,
1572   consistently presented at the beginning during training, is permuted during testing, causing BERT to
1573   misidentify its position. In contrast, our method employs a permutation-invariant approach to graph
1574
       processing, thereby preserving its high performance during testing.
1575
1576
       E.4   L OCAL B LOCK A NALYSIS
1577
       The assessment of the node structure annotation embeddings in HLM-G reveals intriguing insights into
1578   the model’s encoding capabilities. These embeddings, derived from 1-hop neighborhood information,
1579   prompt an inquiry into the model’s approach to capturing such local graph structures. Specifically,
1580   we investigate the positional and structural awareness exhibited by these embeddings, akin to the
1581   strategies employed in GNNs and GTs, where Positional Encoding (PE) (Dwivedi et al., 2022) is a
1582   common technique for enhancing model performance. PE assigns similar positional values to nodes
1583   in close proximity, reflecting their relative positions within the graph.
1584   To evaluate the positional and structural encoding prowess of HLM-G, we create over 10000 pairs of
1585   nodes and analyze the node structure annotation embeddings generated by the lower layers of the
1586   model. By comparing these embeddings using cosine similarity, we categorize the pairs into three
1587   groups based on their hop distance: 1-hop neighbors, 2-hop neighbors, and neighbors at 3 or more
1588   hops.
1589
1590
       Table 14: Cosine similarity of 1-hop and 2-hop neighbors with different numbers of common
       neighbors. We see that cosine similarity between 1-hop and 2-hop neighbours is quite high and
1591
       keeps on increasing with increasing number of common neighbors.
1592
1593
                           Common Neighbors        1-hop Neighbors     2-hop Neighbors
1594
1595                       1                             0.956                 0.931
1596                       3                             0.957                 0.939
1597                       5                             0.966                 0.954
1598
                           7                             0.972                 0.951
                           9                             0.975                 0.968
1599
1600
1601   Table 15: Similarity for 3-hop neighbors (no common neighbors) based on the difference of
1602   structure. The table suggests that lower block assigns similar embedding to nodes that share a
1603   common 1-hop structure.

                                 6                                    -0.135

1614   Table 14 and Table 15 reveals a consistent trend in similarity: embeddings of 1-hop neighbors
1615   exhibit higher similarity compared to those of 2-hop neighbors, and likewise for 3+ hop neighbors.
1616
       Furthermore, we observe that the number of common neighbors between two nodes significantly
       influences the similarity of their embeddings. A higher number of common neighbors indicates
1617
       greater positional similarity between the nodes.
1618
1619   In the case of 3+ hop neighbors where no common neighbors exist, we evaluate the role of structural
       similarity. Here, nodes are considered similar in structure if they share a similar 1-hop neighborhood,


                                                         30
       Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2025


1620
       specifically in terms of the number of neighbors. The analysis demonstrates that the greater the
1621   difference in 1-hop structure between nodes, the lower the similarity in their embeddings. This
1622   suggests that HLM-G effectively encodes 1-hop neighborhood information, assigning higher similarity
1623   to nodes that are either positionally or structurally similar.

1625
1626   F     A BLATION S TUDIES
1627
1628   F.1   P OOLING M ECHANISMS
1629
1630   In the process of constructing node embeddings from the outputs (HU AE , HU X ) of the lower layer,
1631   we examine two distinct pooling mechanisms: mean pooling and concatenate pooling.
1632   Mean pooling employs a parameter α, which signifies the relative importance attributed to structural
1633   information. Specifically,
1634
1635                        zv = Pool(HU AE , HU X ) = α ∗ HU AE + (1 − α) ∗ HU X
1636
       Essentially, each neuron within zv encapsulates both structural and feature information. An α > 0.5
1637
       indicates a predominance of structural information in the final prediction process, whereas α < 0.5
1638
       suggests that nodal features hold greater significance.
1639
1640
1641   Concatenate pooling, in contrast, yields node embeddings of doubled dimensionality by concatenating
1642
       structural and feature embeddings,
1643
                                zv = Pool(HU AE , HU X ) = concat(HU AE , HU X )
1644
1645   This approach integrates structural and feature vectors, thereby expanding the representational
1646   capacity of the resultant node embeddings. The impact of various pooling ratios (α) is systematically
1647   evaluated across one node-level, link-level and graph-level real-world datasets.
1648
1649   Table 16: Performance comparison between mean pooling and concatenate pooling across node-
1650   link- and graph- level datasets. ↑ α implies more structural information is used for making final
1651   predictions. Metric is Accuracy for cora and ROC-AUC for molhiv and PubMed. α = 0 implies only
1652   node features are used for making final prediction whereas α = 1 means that predictions rely entirely
1653   on the graph’s structure.
1654
1655                          Pooling                  Cora      molhiv      PubMed
1656                                            α      Node      Graph        Link
1657                                            0.0    86.32       73.8         95.7
1658                                            0.1    87.06       74.2         94.8
1659                                            0.2    88.45      75.5          96.2
1660                          Mean              0.3     86.9      76.39         97.2
1661                                            0.4     86.3       74.2        97.4
1662                                            0.5     85.9       74.5        98.24
1663                                            0.6    85.58       75.6         98.2
1664                                            1.0    66.35       75.1         91.1
1665
1666                          Concatenate       -      85.35       75.1         96.6
1667
1668   Table 16 shows that mean pooling generally outperforms concatenate pooling, with α values between
1669   0.1 and 0.5 delivering consistently strong results across all datasets. It’s crucial to recognize that α
1670   measures the structural relevance in the final model. Our findings suggest that features specific to
1671   individual nodes are more significant than broader structural characteristics, especially in citation
1672   networks such as Cora, where α = 0.1 is optimal. Conversely, for the PubMed link dataset, an
1673   α value of 0.5 yields the best performance, reflecting the importance of structural connections in
       conveying critical information about the relationships between nodes.


                                                         31
       Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2025


1674
       F.2   I NPUT P ROMPT D ESIGN
1675
1676   Various prompt designs can be employed to describe graph structures for language models. While
1677   the main paper predominantly used a natural language description focusing on 1-hop neighbors (our
1678   Current Graph Description Language, CGDL), it’s important to assess whether different prompt
1679   styles impact the model’s performance. In this ablation study, we explore two additional prompt
1680   styles: the Adjacency List Format (Adj-List) and Edge List Format (Edges).
1681   Moreover, we investigate the model’s out-of-domain (OOD) capabilities under two scenarios:
1682
1683          • Cross-Prompt Evaluation: In this setting, models trained on one prompt design (e.g.,
1684            CGDL) are evaluated on different prompt designs (e.g., Adj-List or Edges) to test adaptabil-
1685            ity.
1686          • Node Token Variability: We introduce OOD test sets where node identifiers are replaced
1687            with random strings of up to four characters, simulating a situation where node tokens during
1688            inference differ from those encountered during training.
1689
1690   Experimental Setup: We conducted our experiments on the Cycle graph reasoning dataset, where we
1691
       trained separate models using each of the three prompt designs—CGDL, Adj-List, and Edges. Each
       model was trained independently using the respective prompt format to ensure it could learn the graph
1692
       structures as described by that particular design. Following training, these models were evaluated
1693
       on all three prompt formats, as well as their OOD versions with altered node tokens, resulting in a
1694   comprehensive assessment of both in-domain and out-of-domain performance. This setup allowed us
1695   to rigorously test the adaptability and robustness of our model under varying prompt styles and node
1696   token representations.
1697
1698   Table 17: In-domain and Out-of-domain performance analysis across different prompt styles
1699   and node token variations on cycle dataset. Performance is measured as accuracy (%).
1700
1701     Training \Testing    CGDL      Adj-List   Edges     CGDL-OOD        Adj-List-OOD      Edges-OOD
1702     CGDL                 99.5%      52.5%     54.1%        96.0%            51.9%           53.6%
1703     Adj-List             93.2%      98.5%     74.2%        73.2%            94.2%           66.5%
1704     Edges                94.5%      86.0%     99.0%        89.5%            78.1%           98.7%
1705
1706   Key Observations:
1707
1708         1. Strong In-Domain Performance: The diagonal entries in Table 17 (99.5%, 98.5%, and
1709            99.0%) indicate that each model performs exceptionally well when evaluated using the same
1710
                prompt style as the one it was trained on, demonstrating strong in-domain performance.
                This suggests that our model is capable of effectively learning graph structures regardless of
1711
                the chosen prompt style.
1712
1713         2. Resilience to Node Token Variability: When examining the OOD results where node
1714            tokens were changed (CGDL-OOD, Adj-List-OOD, Edges-OOD), each model retained
1715
                considerable accuracy compared to its in-domain results. For example, the model trained
                on the Edges format maintained a high performance of 98.7% in the Edges-OOD setting.
1716
                This suggests that the model is robust against variations in node tokens and can maintain its
1717
                graph structure understanding even when faced with different node representations.
1718
1719
             3. Superior Generalization with Edge Descriptions: The model trained with the Edges
                format demonstrated remarkable generalization ability across both cross-prompt settings
1720
                and OOD scenarios. It achieved high accuracy when tested on different prompt designs (e.g.,
1721
                94.5% on CGDL and 86.0% on Adj-List), and similarly performed well even when node
1722            tokens were altered. This indicates that training on the Edges format enables the model to
1723            adapt more effectively to variations in graph description languages and node representations,
1724            making it a versatile choice for different graph tasks.
1725
1726   Overall, this ablation study on input prompt design reveals that our model can handle different
1727   input prompt designs and adapt to node token variations, showcasing its strong generalizability and
       robustness in capturing graph structures across diverse graph description languages.


                                                        32
       Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2025


1728
       F.3   L OCAL B LOCK D ESIGN
1729
1730   In this section, we examine different architectural approaches for the local block of our model,
1731   focusing on how structure and node features are processed. Traditionally, these features are han-
1732   dled hierarchically, meaning they are processed independently from each other. The input to the
1733   hierarchical design lower block is structured as follows:
1734
                   UG = (concat(U1X , U1AE ), concat(U2X , U2AE ), . . . , concat(UnX , UnAE ), UQ )
1735
1736   This approach employs a single lower block, ML , which processes the concatenated features hierar-
1737   chically.
1738
       Following the hierarchical model, we introduce a double hierarchical design, which further divides
1739
       the handling of node and structural features. In this enhanced setup, we implement two distinct lower
1740   blocks: ML1 for node features and ML2 for structural features. The input for this double hierarchical
1741   design is given by:
1742
1743                         UG = concat(U AE v1 , U X v1 , · · · , U AE vn , U X vn , QG )
1744
       This arrangement allows each lower block to specialize, thereby enhancing their processing capabili-
1745
       ties on their respective feature types.
1746
1747   Table 18: Comparison of Model Performance by Design Configuration. Accuracy is used as the
1748   metric. This table presents performance metrics across different datasets, distinguishing between
1749   Hierarchical and Double Hierarchical design models.
1750
1751                                       Double Hierarchical Design            Hierarchical Design
1752               Dataset      Type    1 Lower Block 2 Lower Blocks               1 Lower Block
1753
                  Pubmed       Node          94.25                93.9                   92.9
1754
                   Cora        Node           87.8                88.3                   86.1
1755              WN18RR       Link           97.6                97.5                   97.3
1756
1757
       From Table 18, we note a slight performance advantage with the double hierarchical design. This
1758
       design enables the model to more effectively differentiate between node features and structural
1759
       elements, as these are processed independently in the input, leading to improved performance. The
1760   double hierarchical design exhibits comparable results whether using one or two lower blocks.
1761   Given the similar performance outcomes, we opt for a single lower block due to its lower parameter
1762   count—using two blocks would nearly double the parameters from 86M to 150M. Therefore, in
1763   scenarios where parameter efficiency is critical, the double hierarchical design with a single lower
1764   block is preferable.
1765
1766
       G     L IMITATIONS , C HALLENGES , AND P ERSPECTIVES
1767
1768
       G.1    L IMITATIONS
1769
1770   The most significant limitation of our current methodology lies in its lack of zero- and few-shot
1771   learning capabilities. Recent advancements in Large Language Models (LLMs) have shown excep-
1772   tional proficiency in zero- and few-shot scenarios, suggesting an urgent need for research aimed
1773   at integrating these capabilities into our approach. An initial attempt to address this, described in
1774   Appendix D.2, involves using our model as an encoder coupled with a powerful LLM decoder through
1775   prefix tuning. While this approach enhances fine-tuning efficacy, it falls short in generalizing few-shot
1776   abilities.
1777   Powerful decoder based LLMs can be used in the future leveraging a similar local to global architec-
1778   ture (using similar attention masks). Earlier layers can be set to focus on tokens of the same node
1779   (mimicking intra-node attention). Due to the Causal attention used in decoder LLMs, the last token
1780   in every node’s description can be either directly be used as the node token in upper block or after
1781   pooling with other tokens of same node, mimicking inter-node attention. However, more research is
       needed and we leave this to future work.


                                                          33
       Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2025


1782
       G.2   C HALLENGES
1783
1784   A notable challenge in enhancing our model involves rethinking the attention mechanisms employed
1785   in LLMs. Our model benefits from a unique local and global attention scheme, which could inform
1786   modifications to the attention masks in LLMs. For example, adapting Transformer block architectures
1787   within LLMs to split the layers into two distinct blocks—one focusing exclusively on prior tokens of
1788   the current node (lower block) and the other emphasizing a single embedding for every node (upper
1789   block)—could be a strategy. However, this structural modification is complex to code and train on
1790
       LLMs, and it demands substantial computational resources and algorithmic innovation.
1791
1792   G.3   P ERSPECTIVES
1793
       Hybrid models that combine the structure analysis of Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) with the
1794   language skills of Large Language Models (LLMs) show great promise for creating stronger systems.
1795   These models use the broad abilities of LLMs to work well across different areas, helping to overcome
1796   the specific limitations of traditional GNN architectures. Such models are suited for a wide range of
1797   graph-related tasks in real-world settings, compensating for the limitations of GNNs, which usually
1798   have only a few million parameters and don’t always perform consistently across different fields. This
1799   issue highlights the need for better encoding mechanisms that can represent graph data effectively,
1800   whether it’s for knowledge graphs, molecular structures, or social networks.
1801   Despite increasing interest and some early successes, there are still major challenges, especially in
1802   making these models work well across very different areas. Most current research focuses on node
1803   classification tasks, which don’t fully show what these hybrid models can do in broader applications.
1804   Additionally, tests of these models on various graph reasoning tasks are rare, and the results haven’t
1805   yet shown major breakthroughs. This points to a clear need for more focused research to truly
1806   understand these models’ abilities to interpret complex structures, identifying it as a key area for
1807   future developments.
1808   In conclusion, while our model introduces innovative solutions to graph data analysis, the path
1809   forward involves addressing its scalability to zero-shot learning, enhancing its integration with
1810   LLM architectures, and expanding its adaptability to diverse and complex graph structures. These
1811   developments will not only advance the theoretical foundations of graph neural networks but also
1812   expand their applicability and effectiveness in practical scenarios.
